speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Myra Slotnick. This is tape number four. I'm interviewing Harold Blitzer on September 5th, 1996 in Las Vegas, Nevada. Harold, I want to clarify your experiences when you drove the horse cart and you picked up bodies. Can you please tell me what happened at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: What the procedure was? Yeah. Let me-- let me clarify this. The-- the procedure was they-- they had what they call a burial detail. These people had looked like a funeral truck, you know, like you know, it was black. And they-- they pushed it and pulled it themselves. These are the people who collected all the bodies. There was two or three groups doing it. Brought it to a central pickup point, right. And where I was with my-- I had a flatbed cart where-- where they loaded all these bodies up who were at that time already into square boxes. So I took as many as there was, sometime a couple of hundred, in two or three trips. And the season-- the season decided how many people died. Now, these people died mainly out of starvation or of diseases and things like that. We had no gas chambers in Theresienstadt. I hope that clarifies. I might have made a mistake saying that, yeah. I didn't pick them up. I brought them to the crematorium, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me now about after the liberation, what you did and the circumstances under how long you worked in Denmark and under what circumstances did you come to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Well, when I was at the-- at this orphanage--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you at the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was-- what was it, '45? I had to be, like, still 18, yeah. And-- but this was not an orphanage, this was an orphanage for teenagers sort of. You didn't have any young children there. These are the people who worked as apprentices over there and so forth. I got a job in the-- in the-- in the Harbor District at an automobile-- the place was [NON-ENGLISH] Auto Repair of something like that. And all I did was the typical work for the first seven, eight, nine months. All I did was cleaning up and helping people. I didn't do nothing, I didn't learn anything to speak of. And I was kind of fortunate because about six, seven months down the line, the accountant took a liking to me and he used to invite me over to dinner at his house. And-- and one day he says to me, you know, I got an extra spare room. My wife and my son would love to have you staying at-- at my house. So prior to that, one day I'm in-- we had dormitories where we slept and then we had a little room there with, like, 10 guys. We had lockers and we had a table around with-- with benches. That's where we hang around. And there was a PA system. All of a sudden, I get a PA message, Harold Blitzer, you have a visitor. So now I go downstairs and it's-- it's a Israel soldier. He has a-- has a British uniform but he's got Palestine written up here. And I didn't spoke Hebrew, I could hardly speak any English at all. But we got sort of along. He tells me, here's a letter from your father. And there was packages of 400 cigarettes, Palestinian cigarettes. Because the reason cigarettes was that was the currency after the war in Europe, cigarettes. Everything was based on cigarettes, which is amazing but they didn't know about cancer. But those years, bread than Germany-- it was-- it was all based on cigarettes. So even in our camp, we had the-- the currency was dollars. It was figured out that a bread was so much and a-- and a-- it was either in dollars or cigarettes, although we weren't allowed to smoke and we weren't allowed to-- and probably nobody had any dollars. But the currency was set, the value of certain things and camp was set on-- on-- on cigarettes and dollars. So now I get the letter and I find out for the first time my father is alive. So he also found me to the Red Cross. Within a short time, I get a letter from my father's cousin in the United States. Her name was Sabina Miller. And she asked me if I would like to come to the United States. Oh, by the way, I got that letter before I heard from my father. So now I-- I-- that was-- so I said, yes, I would love to go. So within a very short time, I-- I was sent-- I got an affidavit. Matter of fact, I think that was like within 30 or 90 days, like in June of '45, like a month or so afterwards, she sends me the affidavit from another relative of ours who was very rich, had a bunch of department stores. His there was Sam-- Samuel Jonas. And he owned like 16 department stores in New York and New York City, right on 14th Street. I went-- so now-- [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --I get the-- I get the affidavit, I rush it down to the American embassy in Copenhagen. And at that time, I couldn't understand-- we had a-- we had a joke about-- about those three survivors who talk about where would you like to go? And the first guy, I'm going to Canada and the other guy says, I'm going to Australia. And the third guy says, doesn't anybody want to go to the United States? And then one guy says, well, they have a new way of getting you in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: They put you on a scale and on the other hand, they put paper. And if the paper reaches your weight, that's when you qualify. And then weren't all wrong. When I went to the embassy, I received two documents were probably as long-- as high as the ceiling all the way to the floor. It took me days to fill them out. They wanted-- I mean, they asked for things which were impossible. They wanted the German police department in Berlin to give me a-- if I was a good person. Now, how can you get the-- this is 1945, the war has been over two months and they're asking questions like unbelievable. It took me days to fill it out, right. So now I finally have everything filled out, they call me back again, this has to be filled out, this was wrong, and so forth. So I go back, like, eight, nine, 10 times. So finally, they assign me a quota number. Now, listen to this. The German quota-- I get the German quota this time-- there's nobody from Germany can go to the United States because you have that Nazi thing, right. I'm a Jew, so I was-- I had a very good quarter, but it took over one year for me to get a visa, all right. I finally got ready in November 22nd, 1946. I was ready to go to the United States. And believe it or not, they were right about the paper. There had-- they demanded so much paper. And I had a feeling that don't really want you. So-- and this is how-- really what happened. So that's how I got to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do once you came?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Once- I was picked up my father's cousin at the boat and I arrived there-- oh, was-- oh, was it cold-- December 2nd, 1946. I saw the lady, as we-- the liberty, Statue of Liberty. We got up at three o'clock in the morning to see it. And it was very easy at that time. We didn't have to go through Ellis Island. Ellis Island was already closed at the time because it was no longer necessary, you had all the papers before and you had all the medical things and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got off the boat real fast. There was an immigration, they stamped the thing. And my cousin was waiting for me. They had a sign because I didn't know them, they didn't know what I looked like. And they picked me up and they drove me over to New Jersey. That's where they were living, a place called West New York, New Jersey, which was right across the river. And within days, I-- I had a job. I was working for a German automobile repair shop, not a Jewish German, a real German. And he hired me at $15 a week. Even-- even-- even in those days, that was no money. But he also told me he would pay all my taxes. It took me only the week to find out you only pay taxes after $25. So I worked for him for about three weeks, right. And-- and it was five and a half days a week. We worked almost till two o'clock on Saturdays, right. And so one Saturday I said to him, I'm sorry, I'm leaving. He says, why are you leaving? I says, I-- I don't get enough money.

speaker: interviewee
text: $15 is not enough money. He says, well, I'll give you another $10. I says, no, I-- when I make up my mind, I'm leaving. So I go home and was-- it was in walking distance, like, two or three blocks. I go home and, you know, you lay in the bathtub for-- in those years you didn't have that cleaning, the soap you have today where you can get-- I-- I scrubbed my fingers. I said, I never want to be dirty again, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this guy comes knocking on my door and I-- I finally get out of the bathtub. He says, I will go as high as $50. Then he comes back and says, I'll give you $75. I says, I wouldn't work for you if you give me $200 a week. And I-- that-- that's all-- that's all I said. He gave me his $20-- the $15 I had coming-- and I left. I went to work for a brake outfit for-- and I did-- all of a sudden, I was making $150 because it was a court job, you now, for the peacemen.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and that's all we did-- brakes, brakes. And I was making like $120, $110. One week I had $170. This was a lot of money then, you know. So I was paying off my cousin for the ticket and she charged me $10 a month for a room or something like that, which was-- oh, $10. I paid her every-- every-- no. I paid her every-- something like I paid off the ticket real fast. The ticket was $180, she sent me $20 which I didn't get, it was screwed up. I had to borrowed $10 from a guy on the boat. He lent me-- and he never thought I was going to send-- I sent him the $10. Because he didn't have no money. I couldn't buy a pack of cigarettes, I had nothing. So-- so I--

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no monies for you through any Jewish organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: None whatsoever. We never got anything. We got-- we had some volunteers came on board, somebody of the rich Jewish people who had clothing stores delivered us clothing, you know, because-- see, don't forget, we were in the minority. Most of these Danish people, they had families, they had-- you know, so it wasn't-- we were, like, only, like, about 50 or 60 guys, you know. That's all we were. And so I've got to tell you this first. About six years ago, '89 I think it was-- six, seven years ago-- I was invited back to Berlin, you know, as a-- you know, the City of Berlin invited certain people who was-- whatever. And I was not 65, but that-- that was waived because I was in a concentration camp so they waived the age limit. So we got to Germany and it was right before Passover and they really treated us nice. And of course, the one thing they never said, I'm sorry. They said, well, look, how are we doing now and things like this. And-- so-- but Passover is coming up, so I call up Copenhagen. I figured I'm going to do the same tour. [LAUGHS] I did-- so I went to call up Copenhagen, I talked to the synagogue. I says, I was one of your Danish children, you know-- German children, it was in Denmark. I would appreciate it if I could spend Passover with you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, you know, I said, can I buy a ticket? She said, don't worry about, you know. We will have-- ready for us. I arrived in the morning. I-- I took a night train. By the way, I took the same train we took in 1940. The difference was this time I took a sleeper, I was going first class. [LAUGHS] The other time, we went third class with the wooden benches, right. So I got to Copenhagen like in the morning and I went into a hotel and-- and then in the evening I went to the synagogue and the rabbi. I got there early and we shook hands. New rabbi. They had a different rabbi now, young-- young person. And after service, the rabbi and myself-- I forgot a lot of Danish so we talked in English-- and he said to me-- we were walking to the Jewish center and he says, I hope you forgive us. I says, forgive you for what? He says, we forgot to notify you. See, the Jewish community was warned by the Danish underground because one German official notified that they're going to pick up the Jews. All right? They didn't-- they didn't notify us. And I looked at him and I said, I wish you wouldn't have told me that because I just thought it was bad luck, you know. But that you forgot to notify, that puts another picture on us.

speaker: interviewee
text: If we were notified, if we were told-- see, they told-- the rabbi told-- on the podium he, says, hide, go-- go with some friends, but don't go to your apartments. And he volunteered to go back. He-- he was-- he stayed in his house because he said, I'm not going to leave and leave my people there. And he went-- went with them. So they forgot to notify us. I had to put that in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So let's go back to your experiences in America.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like I said, I quit this-- my first job, I quit and went to work for a braking job. And all of a sudden, like about a month or so later, my real uncle-- I knew I had an uncle here, my-- my mother's brother. And he pops in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, he was here before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: He lived here, came here in 1932 or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Simon Weber, all right. So now-- now, this is my closest relative. I mean, this is my mother's brother, all right. so now-- he-- he looked-- he says, what do you want to do, get dirty? He says, why-- why don't I get you a job? He worked in the garment district. He said, I'll get you a job over there. And he-- I-- says, I've got a friend owns a restaurant, maybe get you a job as a cashier there, all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now I was for it because it was, you know, you really were-- you looked in those years really not just the hands were dirty, but they got chopped up. You looked like blisters and the nails were broken and things like that, you know. So I said, this is a new country, let me get a clean job. So I got a-- so I got a job in this place on 24th Street and Sixth Avenue. It was called Buzzy's Restaurant and Tavern. He had like a bar on that side he had like a-- like a luncheonette on the other side.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I had the job of a cashier. And on Saturday, we had a special cashier booth during the week, and then on Saturday he pulls the cash register over on the other side and I worked also the counter and I made some tips then. So one Saturday, the dishwasher comes to me, a black guy, and says I'm the-- he says, I'm the union-- I'm the union whatever the-- shop steward, the union shop steward.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, would you like to join the union? I says, well, what does it cost? He said, well, it costs $50. I said, I don't have $50. He said, don't worry about it. We take it out like $5 a month or something like that. I says, well, what does it do for me? He says, well, minimum scale is $75 a week. I said, you're kidding. Oh, yeah. So I said, yeah. where do I sign? The next Monday-- the following Monday my boss comes in and who there already, the business agent from the union. He says, he now gets $75 a week. And the guy comes, what are you-- what did you join the union for? I says, I don't know what I was doing. So now he closed the register over on that side. Now he says, this is your job on this side six days a week.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was getting seven-- and now I hours making tips also. So it turned out to be good job. So I stayed there for a while. And then somebody told me about Florida. He says, I'm going down to Florida. By the way, my-- my cousin, I lived with her and she-- one day I came home in the beginning with a-- with a-- with the Jewish paper, [NON-ENGLISH], in German. She took the paper, ripped it apart, you know, you will learn English, and she enrolled me right in the school. And I went to the school and I-- I started meeting people, you know, we went-- and I started learning English. I also went to movies every day on a Saturday, like the first movies I didn't understand, double features. By the time they got around the third time, I could understand English. So that was my-- my English lessons on the weekend. I think movies in those years were like $0.25. I sat there about nine hours sometimes. And so I'm glad I-- I-- I learned English, you know, because it has-- it's helpful. I walk around here with hardly any accent, you know. I mean, my accent is better than Kissinger's and he got here before me. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do when you were in Florida?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to Florida. I went to Florida with this guy a real funny story. This guy had-- he wouldn't-- he had a new Studebaker, that's-- remember, they looked like from the front and the back was the same thing. And-- and he-- I didn't even have to put in gas he had so much money, this guy. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot. I really-- no, I don't remember the name. And it was one of the Jewish guys I met at a Jewish Community Center-- American, you know. I went to Florida and for a couple of days, I stayed with him at the-- he was staying at an ocean front hotel that. I said, what am I, crazy? So I-- I said, I gotta move out because I have a couple of dollars but I didn't have much. But you know, I says, even living in a hotel without costing, you're tipping and this thing and-- so I said, I've got to get a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got a job as a busboy in one of the restaurants. I got a place on South Beach, you know, where this is now the-- the ultra chic section. At that time-- at that time, it was the poor section on Ocean Drive. I remember the street-- I remember I saw the house a couple of years ago, I was down in Florida and took a look at the house. It's still standing. It was a broken down [LAUGHS] place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I stayed there. I think was like $15 a week, two people in a room each paid $15. And then when the season starts, you've got to get kicked out. So now we had to move again. And so I-- so now I worked as a busboy, one of the hardest jobs I ever had, [INAUDIBLE]. I'll never forget that restaurant. That was-- it was a couple, Fannie and Bill. They're very famous as restaurateurs. And they're only open-- like in those years, you only open up in the winter time, you know. So the season started and I finally got my first automobile. It was a 1936 Buick. [LAUGHS] It was the longest thing ever. It was-- with the wheels, with the spare wheels on both sides, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was 11 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your car was 11.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. But-- so whenever I pulled in the gas station for a dollar's worth of gas, I had to have $1 worth of oil because it used as much oil as-- [LAUGHS] so, but that was my first car. And it looked very impressive. It was black. [LAUGHS] I always said, that's my gangster car, you know. And so then I said, I have been enough of a busboy. I says, I'm going to become a waiter.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went around to the different hotels and I went to a place called the Robert Richter Hotel, which was at that time a restricted hotel. I didn't know what restricted means. It was strictly like the advertising said, churches nearby. At that time, that meant Jews were not allowed in that hotel. And so I went to see the head, but it turned out to be a Dutchman. So we started talking Germans and Dutch, I knew a little Dutch, too, because I learned it from some-- some of the Dutch Jews in camp. So he liked me. He says, you know cocktails? I said, of course I know. I didn't have idea what a cocktail was. [BACKROUND NOISE] He says, we're going to put you in there in the bar. You go-- I had the easiest job. At night, during dinner hour, I went upstairs to the dining room-- can I take your drink order, Martini, whatever it was? Went down the bar, picked it up, and got tipped. I Made a lot of money compared to all the waiters had-- had to wait to give them a full course meal before they got a dollar. So-- so I-- and then at night after dinner, I was the only waiter. We had-- you're not going to believe-- we had Pupi Campo with his orchestra there. And at that time, he just started. Then he went over to the Saxony later on, you know. So he was in the bar, it was a nice bar and we had, you know, in those years you didn't have that many different drinks. But he had Cubas-- Cuba libras and old fashioned martinis and Manhattan, right? That was the-- or Singapore sling, you know. [LAUGHS] So I had a great job. So at the end of the season, this is those places close down like in April. I meet a-- there was a dining room waiter that says to me, he says, I got a job in-- in-- in Cincinnati, Ohio, over on the other side of the river in Covington, he says, where the gaming is. He says, I-- want to come with me? I'm going to be the head waiter. I'll make you a captain. I always admired captains. They were getting money, you know. [LAUGHS] I didn't know if Cincinnati captains don't make any money, right? So now-- [LAUGHS] so now he starts and we go to Cincinnati. This guy got a car and I drive with him. You know, I didn't-- I-- I sold my car, you know. I think I gave it away or something or whatever. It was $200 I paid for it, I think I got $50 from somebody. And so I drive this guy up to Cincinnati, I get into a hotel like a weekly hotel, and we go to work the next day, you know, like the place is just opening up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he is there three days and he says, I got a better job, I'm leaving. So they made me the head waiter. [LAUGHS] I have-- I've been in this country less than a year, all of a sudden, I'm a head waiter. The place was called the New Lookout Place [LAUGHS] and it was a great place. No money, I got like $80 bucks. There was no tips because it was in those places you didn't tip for-- for tables and things like that. And I stayed there for a while and then I got in trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened was I was not aware-- well, we had a boss there. There was a-- was a bunch of brothers by the name of [INAUDIBLE] brothers. Is it all right to mention names or something? Yeah. And they were-- I found out later they were like the-- the-- the mob there in Cincinnati. And they controlled those gaming places, right? So we had a rule if one of their people come in, they got a 50% discount but not on steaks, right? So we had those two guys come in one time and-- and the waitress come to me. I had to OK every check. And-- and she presented the check and they're-- these guys scratch it out and put in 50%. And I scratch it out again and send it back. So finally she gets mad. She says, you go over. And I says, gentleman? He says, on steak. [LAUGHS] I was told by Mr. [? Sobal ?] we cannot give any discounts.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not allowed to give you a discount. So the guy pulls out a .45. [LAUGHS] And I'll never forget, it had a white handle on it, one of those white-handled .45s, big gun, you know. And I run, I just took off. And I go by the blackjack table and the-- and the dealer throws me a set of keys, take my car and take off. He said, take off. I said, I was going to-- in a tuxedo, I didn't give a damn. I just-- the guy said, just take off. Took my car, went to the hotel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next morning, the brother comes over, [INAUDIBLE] brother. He says, I'm sorry. What happened? He says, it's all been straightened out. I says, you know, I was very nervous yesterday. [LAUGHS] I says, I-- I appreciate everything you have done for me, but I am not going to go back there. [LAUGHS] I'm just going to bring the car back tonight and I hope you [LAUGHS] understand this because I'm still very chicken.

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So they opened up a new hotel in-- do you really want to know all this stuff-- opened up a hotel called Terrace Plaza. On top of the-- on top of the roof, they had a small restaurant that only seated 50 people. And they were looking for waiters who can operate this kind of service, which all of a sudden, I became an expert on. And-- and the opening was-- and the dress where they gave us a tuxedo but we had to provide the shirts. And the shirts had to be ring collars. But in those years, you couldn't find-- today you can-- everybody has these shirts. So finally, I find a place where they sell you these paper [LAUGHS] collars. And you had to have a shirt separate, you know. That's that's what they demanded, you know. Well, the opening night the air condition cracks out, right, [LAUGHS] and all of a sudden, my collar pops. [LAUGHS] So I go in the kitchen and I worked with a partner there and I made sure everything was-- and I took my jacket off and I didn't go in the dining room anymore because I didn't have a collar. The thing just popped open. So now-- and those tuxedos were real heavy, you know. I mean, they really-- you know,  it was baloney to give that much. And it was a disaster, that opening night. That was a disaster. The food didn't go out, the kitchen wasn't ready, the whole thing. But it took-- it got straightened out. We didn't have to wear big collars anymore. We got a regular tuxedo shirts. So-- but it was a good job.

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed there till-- till next season and I went back to Florida. And you know, I became one of those snowbirds, you know. The minute it got cold, go back to Florida. And at that time, I worked in the Saxony Hotel that just opened up, and Pupi Campo moved over there. And little by little, I got a great job at a place called the Greenbriar Hotel in West Virginia, very famous hotel, and it was owned by the railroad tycoon, Robert-- his name was Robert Young. I always thought it was the actor, but it wasn't. And he owned the Chesapeake and Ohio Railroad. And those are well-- well-financed because they did all the coal-- coal transports, you know. And this was a great job, this was. We had quarters there, got everything. We had even a golf course. Unfortunate, I didn't learn. [LAUGHS] I didn't want to play golf. Sam Snead was the pro out there. And I stayed there for about two seasons. [LAUGHS] In September 1950-- [LAUGHS] no, I got-- all of a sudden in July or August, the Korean War starts and I get a notice, greetings. [LAUGHS] So I get nailed and I had to go to Charleston, West Virginia to-- to get my induction, you know. And-- and the minute you say, [LAUGHS] I'm Jewish, 1A. [LAUGHS] You could have flat feet, you can't hear, you're 1A. I mean, I-- I-- so that's how I got into the army for two years, you know. Again, I was very fortunate. I took over an officers club while I was there because I had that background and I survived the-- the two years. I didn't get sent overseas, although there was a number of times I was on orders. But the colonel always took-- because I had a very essential job. I made a profit for the club and that they never had before. I cut out the dues, didn't have to pay dues. I gave them free parties because I had a very corrupt [LAUGHS] mess officer. I hope the army doesn't nail me. But he-- he got nailed, I understand, because he-- they found out at a grocery store in Philadelphia. His name was Captain Harvey, I'll never forget his name. I became initially his personal cook. And I used to make him eggs Benedict and steaks Diane. This is what he-- you know, what I learned as a waiter, you know, and in those kind of places. And so that was my tour in the army. And that's mois. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- OK. I want to-- oh--
